coredata.dc:title: Ammonia, a Switch for Controlling High Ionic Conductivity in Lithium Borohydride Ammoniates
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Tengfei
Wang, Yongming
Song, Tao
Miyaoka, Hikaru
Shinzato, Keita
Miyaoka, Hiroki
Ichikawa, Takayuki
Shi, Siqi
Zhang, Xiaogang
Isobe, Shigehito
Hashimoto, Naoyuki
Kojima, Yoshitsugu
coredata.dc:description: Lithium borohydride ammoniates can be readily generated by LiBH4 absorbing ammonia at room temperature. Li(NH3)nBH4 (0 < n ≤ 2) performed high ionic conductivity near room temperature, e.g., 2.21 × 10−3 S cm−1 for mono-ammoniate at 40°C. A drastic increase in ionic conductivity occurs around 38°C due to the structural change resulting from ammonia desorption. Moreover, the jump of the ionic conductivity is reversible because ammonia absorption/desorption acts as a switch for the structural change. The experimental results show that the stable electrochemical window for the sample is ∼4 V. The crystal structure of Li(NH3)nBH4 (0 < n ≤ 1) can be easily changed while the equilibrium dynamically changes with temperature. First-principles calculation results also indicate that Li(NH3)BH4 exerts an expected compatibility in contact with lithium iron phosphate cathode material. Finally, by introducing a gas instead of substituting an element, a dynamic change of crystal structure is achieved, accompanied with the improvement of lithium ionic conductivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: solid-state electrolyte
lithium borohydride ammoniates
ammonia
high ionic conductivity
first-principles calculations
in situ environmental TEM

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Dr. Ankur Jain and Mr. Hiroki Uesato for their kind help in this work. Special thanks are given to Dr. Peng Tan (PolyU, Hongkong, China), Dr. Liang Zeng, Dr. Jiechen Lu (Tokyo University, Japan), Dr. Kun (Kelvin) Fu (University of Maryland), and Prof. Liangbing Hu (University of Maryland) for their good advice and kind help in this study. T.S. and S.S. acknowledge support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 51622207 and U1630134 ) and Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (grant no. 18010500300 ). All the computations were performed on the high-performance computing platform of Shanghai University. In addition, T.Z. wants to give his sincere love to his wife, Xiaoran Qin and their newborn baby, Jinghang Zhang.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Crystal Structures
(A and B) Mono-ammoniate (A) and LiBH4 (B). Solid-line boxes show each unit cell.
Electrical Properties
(A) Electrical properties of Li(NH3)BH4. Typical Nyquist plots obtained using a lithium foil electrode in heating process from 20°C to 45°C.
(B) The lithium ionic conductivity of Li(NH3)BH4 obtained in heating process (black), cooling process (red), and as-received LiBH4 (blue).
(C) Lithium ionic conductivity of another four samples, Li(NH3)BH4 for the fifth cycle (black), SUS electrode Li(NH3)BH4 (red), Li(NH3)2BH4 (blue), and Li(NH3)0.5BH4 (dark cyan).

(D) Time dependence of DC conductivity of Li(NH3)BH4 after applying a constant voltage of 0.1 V for lithium and SUS electrodes at 40°C, respectively. Inset: a typical impedance plot obtained using an SUS electrode at 35°C.
Lithium Ionic Conductivity
TG-DTA-TDMS Results of Li(NH3)BH4
(A) (1) Mass spectra of H2 gas desorption. (2) Mass spectra of NH3 gas desorption. (3) Temperature program, first increasing to 45°C and maintained for 20 min, finally increasing to 60°C.
(B) (4) Weight loss resulting from NH3 desorption. (5) DTA curve.
PC Isotherms for NH3 Absorption of LiBH4 at 293 K
Cyclic Voltammetry Measurements
Li/Li(NH3)0.5BH4/SUS cell performed at 40°C and scanned at 5 mV s−1 (black); Li/LiBH4/SUS cell performed at 145°C and scanned at 5 mV s−1 (blue).
TEM Images of Samples
(A–E) In situ TEM observation of lithium borohydride mono-ammoniate with an environmental cell. Bright-field images at (A) 20°C, (B) 30°C, (C) 40°C, (D) 50°C, and (E) 60°C.
(F–J) Related diffraction pattern obtained in the blue dashed area 1 for (A) to (E), respectively.
First-Principles Calculation Results of Reaction Products and Maximum Mixing Energies between Li(NH3)BH4 and LiFePO4

head.ce:title: Ammonia, a Switch for Controlling High Ionic Conductivity in Lithium Borohydride Ammoniates

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Materials and Technology for Energy Conversion, College of Material Science and Engineering, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, Nanjing 210016, China

and School of Mechatronic Engineering and Automation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Genome Institute, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, China
Graduate School of Integrated Arts and Sciences, Hiroshima University, Higashi-Hiroshima 739-8521, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate School of Integrated Arts and Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate School of Engineering, Hiroshima University, Higashi-Hiroshima 739-8530, Japan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium borohydride ammoniates can be readily generated by LiBH4 absorbing ammonia at room temperature. Li(NH3)nBH4 (0 < n ≤ 2) performed high ionic conductivity near room temperature, e.g., 2.21 × 10−3 S cm−1 for mono-ammoniate at 40°C. A drastic increase in ionic conductivity occurs around 38°C due to the structural change resulting from ammonia desorption. Moreover, the jump of the ionic conductivity is reversible because ammonia absorption/desorption acts as a switch for the structural change. The experimental results show that the stable electrochemical window for the sample is ∼4 V. The crystal structure of Li(NH3)nBH4 (0 < n ≤ 1) can be easily changed while the equilibrium dynamically changes with temperature. First-principles calculation results also indicate that Li(NH3)BH4 exerts an expected compatibility in contact with lithium iron phosphate cathode material. Finally, by introducing a gas instead of substituting an element, a dynamic change of crystal structure is achieved, accompanied with the improvement of lithium ionic conductivity.

The progress of lithium-ion battery technology has been inseparable from the innovation of electrolyte materials. Compared with the traditional organic liquid electrolytes, stable solid-state electrolytes are safe, easy to assemble, and inexpensive. However, the large-scale application of solid-state electrolytes is currently limited owing to its low ionic conductivity at room temperature. Generally, lithium ionic conductivity of a solid electrolyte is easily influenced by its crystal structure and defect density, rather than others, and the gas absorption/desorption reaction is an easy way to induce the structural change in solids. Here, we first propose a new concept of utilizing the gas absorption/desorption reaction to improve the ionic conductivity of solids, and demonstrate the ionic conductivity jump of lithium borohydride ammoniates switched by ammonia desorption and absorption at around room temperature.

Lithium ionic conductivity up to 10−3 S cm−1 can be achieved from solid-state lithium borohydride ammoniates near room temperature. The gradual change of crystal structure from Li(NH3)BH4 to Li(NH3)xBH4 (0 < x < 1) with the increase of temperature indicates a dynamic equilibrium formed in a closed system. The electrochemical properties of lithium borohydride ammoniates have been investigated. The amount of ammonia as a switch is a key factor controlling the lithium ionic conductivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have attracted much more attention from laboratory to practical industrial applications because of the increasing number of electronic products, mobile phones, and electric vehicles. 1,2 Furthermore, current investigations of LIBs have focused on solid-state electrolytes (SSEs) instead of organic liquid electrolytes in traditional LIBs due to its safety problems, stable solid-electrolyte interface formation, and poor cycling performance. 3 A number of SSEs have been developed for all solid-state LIBs. 4,5 The oxide type compounds, such as perovskite, 6,7 NASICONs, 8,9 and LISICONs, 10,11 exhibit high lithium ionic conductivity on the order of 10−5 to 10−3 S cm−1 at room temperature. The thio-LISICONs (Li2S-P2S5) have been widely investigated from 2001 as its conductivity (1.7 × 10−2 S cm−1) is almost 100 times higher than that in LISICONs, where oxygen is replaced by sulfur. 8–15 LiBH4 exhibits a high lithium ionic conductivity (10−4 S cm−1) at approximately 117°C due to its structural transition from orthorhombic to hexagonal. 16 Because this hexagonal high-temperature phase can be stabilized by forming solid solution phase, the structural distortion maintains the conductivity (∼10−5 S cm−1) at 3 orders of magnitude higher than the orthorhombic phase (10−8 S cm−1) even at room temperature. 17,18 This suggests that phase deformation of LiBH4 could influence its lithium ionic conductivity due to inducing defects and/or changing the atomic arrangement.

More recently, systematic investigation of the ammonia absorption properties of LiBH4 has been conducted to obtain the basic data for designing ammonia storage materials for safe utilization. 19 LiBH4 can easily absorb NH3 and form ammoniates with various compositions, Li(NH3)nBH4 (1 ≤ n ≤ 4). Among them, Li(NH3)BH4, Li(NH3)3BH4, and Li(NH3)4BH4 are solid phases and Li(NH3)2BH4 is a liquid phase in accordance with the LiBH4-NH3 phase diagram reported by Sullivan et al. 20 The crystal structure and dehydrogenation properties of these materials have already been reported by Yu et al. 21–23 and Chen et al. 24 The crystal structure of Li(NH3)BH4 belongs to the orthorhombic system, 21,25 and the same space group as that of its parent compound LiBH4
26 (Figure 1 ).

Li(NH3)BH4 can be characterized by 2-dimensional sheets formed by configuring NH3 molecules into (002) planar of LiBH4. The structural similarity and the characteristics of structural transition between LiBH4 and Li(NH3)BH4 are relevant to the simplicity of NH3 absorption in LiBH4 and desorption from Li(NH3)BH4. Note that the structural transition obviously lowers the volume density of lithium ion in Li(NH3)BH4, because the unit cell volume of Li(NH3)BH4
25 is approximately 1.8 times that of LiBH4. 26 Considering the effect of hexagonal distortion of LiBH4 on its lithium ionic conductivity, it is expected that the structural deformation of LiBH4 by adsorbing NH3 improves its lithium ionic conductivity by inducing defects during the deformation and/or lowering of the activation energy for mobility of lithium ions due to the change in atomic arrangement and the decrease in the volume density of lithium ion. Therefore, in this study we have investigated experimentally and theoretically the lithium ionic conductivities of lithium borohydride ammoniates. Experimental details and methodology on depicting the electrochemical stability of Li(NH3)BH4 are given in Supplemental Information.

As reported in a previous study, 21 the different lithium borohydride ammoniates can readily be synthesized by controlling the amount of provided ammonia at room temperature. The mono-ammoniate has been first investigated as a solid-state electrolyte in this study due to its structural characteristics and its advantage of low vapor pressure (2.2 kPa) at room temperature for practical applications. The lithium ionic conductivity was measured in a frequency range from 1 MHz to 1 Hz using two pieces of lithium foil electrodes as shown in the typical Nyquist plots. Figure 2 A shows the impedance plots obtained from Li(NH3)BH4. Their diameters sensitively decrease with increasing temperature from 20°C to 45°C. In Figure 2B, Li(NH3)BH4 shows a lithium ionic conductivity of 1.5 × 10−6 S cm−1 at 30°C, which is higher than that of LiBH4 (10−8 S cm−1) by 2 orders of magnitude. It is more interesting that when the temperature is higher than 37°C, the ionic conductivity of Li(NH3)BH4 dramatically increases and jumps to 2.21 × 10−3 S cm−1 at 40°C, while the conductivity of LiBH4 almost remains unchanged. A hysteresis in the conductivity between the cooling and heating curves is due to ammonia reabsorption into the sample when the temperature is decreasing from 40°C to 35°C. A new dynamic equilibrium is achieved and remains steady. Moreover, Li(NH3)0.5BH4 was selected to be tested due to its better thermal stability and lower vapor pressure compared with the mono-ammoniate. A similar jump in conductivity was also detected around 37°C in Li(NH3)0.5BH4 with lithium ionic conductivity of 1.29 × 10−3 S cm−1 (Figure 2C). The large jump in conductivity from 10−6 to 10−3 S cm−1 indicates that change in the ammoniates occurred at around 37°C. More specifically, cyclic experiments show that the process is reversible and high lithium ionic conductivity can be maintained at 40°C (Figure 2C, black curve). For the avoidance of doubt, the DC conductivity of the mono-ammoniate was also measured using lithium electrodes and stainless-steel (SUS) electrodes. The DC conductivity for lithium electrodes is around 1.4 × 10−3 S cm−1 after 1,000 s at 40°C, nearly 4 orders of magnitude (1.8 × 10−7 S cm−1) higher than that for SUS electrodes (Figure 2D). Accordingly, the electronic conduction is almost negligible in this study.

To determine the relationship between the leap in conductivity and the effective ammonia absorption amount, we synthesized a series of Li(NH3)xBH4 samples to investigate the property of ammonia content. Figure 3 shows the ionic conductivity results from 20°C to 40°C. The lithium ionic conductivity increases gradually with the increased ammonia absorption from 0 to 0.4. However, the effective leap to 10−3 (S cm−1) level appears when the ammonia amount x is equal to or larger than 0.5, thus the switch is “on” at 40°C. Thus we investigated the thermal desorption properties and conducted structural analysis by thermogravimetry and differential thermal analysis with thermal gas desorption mass spectrometry (TG-DTA-TDMS) and X-ray diffraction (XRD), respectively.

The results of TG-DTA-TDMS are shown in Figure 4 . A very small amount of hydrogen was detected at 45°C (Figure 4A1), corresponding to the reports by other researchers. 20,21 Compared with ammonia, the desorption of hydrogen was so tenuous that it could be negligible. 25 The by-product should have less effect on the lithium ionic conductivity of Li(NH3)BH4 (Figure S2). Figure 4A2 indicates that NH3 desorption starts while the temperature is around 37°C, in agreement with the jump temperature of the ionic conductivity in Figure 2B. It is obvious that the major rise in lithium ionic conductivity of Li(NH3)BH4 at 40°C should be attributed to the desorption of ammonia, which could induce defects and/or lower the mobility activation energy of lithium ion. During temperature gradation up to 50°C, nearly 20% NH3 in Li(NH3)BH4 was desorbed (Figure 4B4) and the sample changed to Li(NH3)0.8BH4. The sharp peak of DTA (Figure 4B5) indicated the melting of Li(NH3)0.8BH4 at 50°C. 20 NH3 was continually released in an appropriate temperature range in the TG-mass spectrometry-DTA measurement. However, in a closed system, such as AC impedance measurement, a partial desorption reaction of Li(NH3)BH4 could proceed as a dynamic equilibrium. The equilibrium of the reaction
Li(NH3)BH4 ⇄ Li(NH3)1−xBH4 + xNH3 (0 < x < 1)

would be attained while NH3 gas pressure becomes its vapor pressure. This equilibrium dynamically changes with temperature because of the temperature dependence of the vapor pressure, and is maintained by absorption/desorption of NH3 accompanied by the change of lithium ionic conductivity. This should be the reason for why the change in the ionic conductivity of Li(NH3)BH4 is reversible between heating and cooling (Figure 2B). We also conducted an in situ differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) experiment in a closed system (Figure S1) for this sample. The Ar gas atmosphere was kept at 1 atm (the same pressure as in the AC impedance test) in a closed cell. From the DSC results, the sample is partially melted at 45.8°C and totally melted at 58.0°C, which is consistent with the results of the LiBH4-NH3 phase diagram reported in the literature. This indicates that the leap in conductivity is related simply to the ammonia desorption, not the melting of the sample.

As shown in Figures S3 and S4, the XRD profiles indicate the formation of Li(NH3)BH4 with fine crystallinity 24 after heating LiBH4 at 40°C in an ammonia atmosphere. The profile of starting material of as-received LiBH4 is compared in Figure S3B. Figure S3C shows the diffraction profile obtained from the sample after AC impedance measurement in a closed system. Another sample of ammonia absorption of 0.5 mol, Li(NH3)0.5BH4, was also synthesized to compare with Li(NH3)BH4 and Li(NH3)xBH4 (0 < x < 1, closed system). The main peak positions of Li(NH3)xBH4 (0 < x < 1, closed system) are the same as that of Li(NH3)0.5BH4, with only the intensity of the peaks being different. The gradual change of crystal structure from Li(NH3)BH4 to Li(NH3)xBH4 (0 < x < 1) with the increase in temperature indicates that a dynamic equilibrium was formed in a closed system. This also proves that the overall change is reversible during temperature decrease and agrees with the cooling process results of lithium ionic conductivity as shown in Figure 2.

To confirm the dynamic equilibrium condition of LiBH4 absorbing NH3, we determined the amount of absorbed NH3 and the corresponding plateau pressure by pressure-composition (PC) isothermal measurements (Figure 5 ). The equilibrium ammonia pressure of Li(NH3)0.5BH4 is 0.1 kPa at 20°C, equivalent to the results in a previous report. 10 Meanwhile, the equilibrium ammonia pressure of Li(NH3)BH4 is 2.2 kPa at 20°C. Both samples show low vapor pressures at 20°C and 40°C. 20 Low vapor pressure indicates that the reaction equilibrium can easily form between Li(NH3)nBH4 (0 < n ≤ 1) and ammonia, meaning an advantage of the materials for practical application as the solid-state electrolyte. The lithium ionic conductivity of the liquid-phase sample of Li(NH3)2BH4 was also measured. Even though Li(NH3)2BH4 has a higher vapor pressure (6.1 kPa) at 20°C, it still has the potential for application due to its high ionic conductivity at room temperature. It is noteworthy that only Li(NH3)2BH4 showed high conductivity (0.7 × 10−3 S cm−1) at 20°C (Figure 2C, blue curve).

Additionally, to assess the electrochemical stability of lithium borohydride ammoniates described above for the development of LIBs, we evaluated the working potential window from the cyclic voltammogram of a Li/Li(NH3)0.5BH4/SUS cell and a Li/LiBH4/SUS cell with a lithium reference electrode at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 and a scan range of −1 to 4.3 V (Figure 6 ). The current evolves linearly with the potential reflecting lithium deposition (negative potential, Li+ + e− → Li) and dissolution (positive potential, Li → Li+ + e−) on the electrodes. The current drops owing to the deposited lithium being removed from the electrode. No additional current due to electrolyte decomposition is observed in the scanned voltage range from −0.5 to nearly 4 V, indicating that this solid electrolyte is more stable than its parent compound in this potential window. Furthermore, the interface stability was estimated by applying a constant current to plate and strip lithium in a symmetric cell. 27,28
Figure S5 shows the voltage profile of the cell cycled at 40°C under a current density of 0.2 mA cm−2. The cell exhibited a relatively stable voltage around 0.025 V when the temperature was 40°C. The DC conductivity calculated from the symmetric cell was 1.24 × 10−3 S cm−1.

Meanwhile, to evaluate the electrochemical stability of Li(NH3)BH4 (i.e., self-decomposition stability and electrochemical compatibility with electrode), we performed two-step calculations: self-decomposition stability and its compatibility stability with LiFePO4, respectively. 29 Relevant details are presented in Tables S1 and S2. On the one hand, Li(NH3)BH4 possesses a 3.71-V stable self-thermodynamic decomposition window, which is consistent with experimental condition of no-drop current near 4.00 V. On the other hand, self-dynamics decomposition analysis also clearly indicates that Li(NH3)BH4 can be kept stable within the range from self-dynamics upper voltage (4.7 V) to the lithium extraction limit (0.1 V). Furthermore, we examined the electrochemical compatibility between Li(NH3)BH4 and LiFePO4 (i.e., the possible interface reactions) by building two interphase models with and without mixing and calculating their maximum mixing energies, as shown in Table 1 . The positive maximum mixing energy indicates that the interfacial reaction can take place spontaneously, resulting in poor interfacial compatibility. It is clearly seen that both interfaces exhibit negative maximum mixing energies (−0.02 eV and −0.46 eV), revealing good electrochemical compatibility between Li(NH3)BH4 and LiFePO4 and the benign cycle performance in such a half-cell system.

Last but not least, an in situ transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation (JEOL JEM-2010, 200 kV) of lithium borohydride mono-ammoniate was performed with an environmental cell holder. The experimental condition was under 1 atm Ar gas atmosphere in a closed system with temperature increasing from 20°C to 60°C. These in situ TEM results are first reported here to show the diffraction pattern transformation and the morphology change of the sample (Figure 7 ). As the temperature increases from 20°C to 50°C, Li(NH3)BH4 desorbs a very small amount of ammonia to achieve a new dynamic equilibrium. The diffraction patterns show that the sample structure is transformed from polycrystalline to single crystal by heat treatment. The crystal diffraction fully disappears at 60°C, and the morphology and contrast of the sample become more homogeneous, indicating that the sample is melting. Therefore, Li(NH3)BH4 would melt when the temperature is higher than 50°C, which is consistent with the LiBH4-NH3 phase diagram reported in the literature. 20 From the TEM and DSC results, the sample during the cycling test would remain in solid state, with the high lithium ionic conductivity obtained at 40°C also in solid state. Considering the TG-mass spectrometry and DTA results together, the sudden jump in the conductivity is only related to the ammonia desorption and the dynamic equilibrium between the amounts of ammonia in gas and solid.

A series of lithium borohydride ammoniates were investigated in this study. During temperature increase, ammonia is desorbed from Li(NH3)nBH4 (0 < n ≤ 1), leaving positions vacant to form Schottky defects. Meanwhile, lithium ion in the immediate vicinity could migrate to one of the vacancies. This would also leave the previous site as a new vacancy for hosting another ion. This process could lead to a continual diffusion pathway of ions and increase ionic conductivity. 30 In this case, Li(NH3)0.5BH4 and Li(NH3)BH4 exhibit high lithium ionic conductivity at 40°C. Li(NH3)BH4 shows a structure transformation from room temperature to 50°C according to the TEM results. The drastically increase in ionic conductivity can also be triggered by the reorientation and the vibration of the anions when the amount of ammonia slightly changes during dynamic equilibrium reformation. 31–34 The conductivity-jump temperature of lithium borohydride ammoniates (37°C) appears much earlier than that of the related borohydrides, such as LiBH4 (120°C), 16 Na2B12H12 (270°C), 35 LiCB11H12 (130°C), 33 and NaCB11H12 (110°C). 33 Besides, Li+ conductivity of lithium borohydride ammoniates exhibits a better performance than that of the glass-ceramics, Li7P3S11, 36 and almost reaches the same level as the best known material, Li10GeP2S12. 15 Furthermore, according to the PCI results, a dynamic equilibrium with low vapor pressure forms during LiBH4 absorption/desorption of NH3 at different temperatures. This implies that high ionic conductivity can be maintained at 40°C. Hence, Li(NH3)nBH4 (0 < n ≤ 1) could be used as SSEs due to their high thermostability and low-equilibrium ammonia vapor pressure. Additionally, Li(NH3)2BH4 has a particularly low melting temperature (around 15°C) 20 and exhibits a high conductivity of 1.75 × 10−3 S cm−1 at 30°C (Figure 2C), and thus is a potential candidate as an inorganic ionic liquid in electrochemical applications. 37

In solid inorganic compounds, ionic transportation mainly depends on the concentration and distribution of defects in the crystal structure. In this case, many solid electrolytes with special structural characteristics are synthesized for all-solid-state batteries, such as NASICON-type, LISICON-type, perovskite-type, and sulfide-type batteries. For NASICON and LISICON types, the lithium ionic conducting behaviors are mainly caused by hopping of lithium ions among interstitial sites of the crystal lattice. The lithium ionic conductivity of perovskite and sulfide types highly relies on their framework structure, which form a lithium conduction pathway along the axis. Among these materials, their crystal structures have remained unchanged since the time they were synthesized. However, the crystal structure of Li(NH3)nBH4 (0 < n ≤ 1) can be easily changed while the equilibrium dynamically changes with temperature. Generally, lithium ionic conductivity of a solid electrolyte is easily influenced by its crystal structure and defect density, rather than other factors, and the gas absorption/desorption reaction is an easy way to induce structural change in solids. Finally, by introducing a gas instead of substituting an element, the dynamic change in crystal structure is achieved, accompanied by improvement in lithium ionic conductivity.

The ability of LiBH4 to absorb ammonia at room temperature could lead to the formation of potentially useful lithium ionic electrolytes, Li(NH3)nBH4 (0 < n ≤ 2). These electrolytes show an extremely high specific conductivity (10−3 S cm−1) at around room temperature. Li(NH3)nBH4 (0.5 ≤ n ≤ 1) could be used as SSEs due to their thermostability, electrochemical stability, and recyclability under 40°C. In addition, Li(NH3)BH4 presents a stable electrochemical window and an expected compatibility with lithium iron phosphate cathode material according to the first-principles calculation. Li(NH3)2BH4, with low viscosity and high conductivity, might be used as an inorganic ionic liquid in practical applications at room temperature. We hope that the findings of this study could propose LiBH4 not only as an important ammonia or hydrogen storage material but also as a superior lithium ionic electrolyte in next-generation batteries. Last but not least, dispensing with traditional thinking, the concept of designing electrolytes through introducing gas to change the crystal structure dynamically alongside improvement in ionic conductivity should be emphasized for future study. Since the concept has only just been proposed and studied, more detailed work needs to be carried out in depth to fully understand this series of materials in future experiments and practical applications.

As starting materials. LiBH4 (Aldrich [≥95%] and Li foil [Honjo Metal]) were used as received. These samples were handled in a glovebox (Miwa, MDB-2BL) filled with purified argon gas to avoid oxygen and water contamination. Li(NH3)nBH4 was prepared by introducing NH3 to LiBH4 at room temperature (Figure S6). Ammonia absorption of lithium borohydride was evaluated by PC isothermal measurements. The apparatus comprised an NH3 cylinder (NH3 purity, 99.999%), buffer, pump, pressure gauge (Druck, DPI280), and sample holder. After absorption, sample was kept at 40°C in an oil bath for 4 hr before cooling down to room temperature. The value for n is calculated by the following formula: n= (weight gain/17.0305)/initial mol of LiBH4. The n value for the prepared sample is 1.01. The main composition for sample was recorded as Li(NH3)BH4. The other two samples, Li(NH3)0.5BH4 and Li(NH3)2BH4, were synthesized by the same method. The amount of NH3 introduced was also evaluated by using ammonia density at each temperature and pressure, referenced from the National Institute of Standards and Technology database.

The powder sample was loaded into stainless-steel vessels with 15-mm inner diameter and pressed into a pellet as solid electrolyte. Two metallic lithium foils of 15 mm diameter were then placed on the two sides of electrolyte as electrodes. For the liquid electrolyte, Polyolefin was used as a separator between the two lithium electrodes. Afterward, the pellet was put into an electrochemical impedance spectrum cell for future impedance measurements. All processes took place in the glovebox filled with Ar gas. The ionic conductivity was measured by AC impedance method in a frequency range from 1 Hz to 1 MHz and DC conductivity with an electrochemical workstation (BioLogic Science Instruments). The cell was placed in an oil bath to control the temperature. Experimental temperature ranges were set from 20°C to 45°C. Lithium ionic conductivity data were evaluated from the diameter of a single arc from the Nyquist plot. The electrochemical stability of Li/Li(NH3)0.5BH4/SUS cell was measured using cyclic voltammetry with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 between −1 and 5 V at 40°C. The lithium stripping/plating cycling used the same assembly method as with the AC impedance test and was measured with an applied current density of 0.2 mA cm−2 at 40°C.

NH3 absorption properties of the as-received LiBH4 were investigated by volumetric technique, which is a PC isothermal measurement (Beljapan, BELSORP-max) at several temperatures. An equilibrium pressure was monitored and determined when the pressure variation was within 0.3% in every 5-min period. The waiting time was suspended until the conditions for the determination were satisfied.

The in situ observation was carried out by an environmental cell holder of a transmission electron microscope (JEOL JEM-2010, at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV) (Figure S7). This holder has a window-type cell, which consists of two window-films to isolate the gas layer (∼0.5 mm thick) from the high vacuum atmosphere of the TEM column. Amorphous SiN thin film of 15-nm thickness was used as the window material and was supported on a single crystal Si disc 0.2 mm thick, 3.5 mm diameter, and with a 0.2 × 0.2-mm central window. The film can support gas pressure to 0.1 MPa inside the cell. The temperature of sample was controlled by a Peltier device in the holder, which allows heating from −30°C to 100°C. Two nozzles for gas injection and evacuation were built into the cell.

Based on the Vienna ab initio simulation package, first-principles calculation within the Projector Augmented Wave formalism was performed. 38 Generalized gradient approximation is applied to deal with the exchange-correlation energy for such a system. 39 To obtain the ground state of Li(NH3)BH4, we built a 5.96 × 4.46 ×14.32-Å unit cell into our calculations. 21 The cutoff energy for the wave function is specified as 520 eV and 3 × 3 × 1 k-meshes are used to sample the Brillouin zone. All of the parameters are carefully tested. Self-consistent calculations are considered to be converged until both the ions and cells are relaxed in optimization with the energy and force convergence criteria of 10−4 eV/supercell and 0.02 eV/Å, respectively. The methodology for characterizing the electrochemical stability of Li(NH3)BH4 is detailed in Supplemental Information.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Dr. Ankur Jain and Mr. Hiroki Uesato for their kind help in this work. Special thanks are given to Dr. Peng Tan (PolyU, Hongkong, China), Dr. Liang Zeng, Dr. Jiechen Lu (Tokyo University, Japan), Dr. Kun (Kelvin) Fu (University of Maryland), and Prof. Liangbing Hu (University of Maryland) for their good advice and kind help in this study. T.S. and S.S. acknowledge support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (nos. 51622207 and U1630134) and Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (grant no. 18010500300). All the computations were performed on the high-performance computing platform of Shanghai University. In addition, T.Z. wants to give his sincere love to his wife, Xiaoran Qin and their newborn baby, Jinghang Zhang.

ce:section.ce:para: T.Z. conceived and designed the experiments. T.Z., Y.W., Hiroki Miyaoka, T.I., S.I., N.H., and X.Z. contributed to the data analysis. T.Z., Y.W., S.S., T.S., and T.I. contributed to manuscript writing. T.Z. and K.S. contributed to the DSC analysis. Hikaru Miyaoka contributed to the PC thermal analysis. T.S. and S.S. contributed to the first-principles calculation. T.Z., Y.W., T.S., Hikaru Miyaoka, K.S., Hiroki Miyaoka, T.I., S.S., X.Z., S.I., N.H., and Y.K. provided valuable feedback.
The authors declare no competing interests.
Supplemental Information includes Supplemental Experimental Procedures, seven figures, and two tables and can be found with this article online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joule.2018.04.015.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Document S1. Supplemental Experimental Procedures, Figures S1–S7, and Tables S1 and S2
Document S2. Article plus Supplemental Information
